color_type.brief: A struct that encapsulates styling attributes for text elements, including identifiers, color, and font weight.
color_type.details: The `color_type` struct represents styling information used for formatting text elements within user interfaces or text processing systems. It contains a `name` for a human-readable identifier, a `code` for programmatic referencing, a `color` value as an integer to specify the display color, and a `bold` flag indicating whether the text should be rendered in bold style. These members enable flexible and consistent styling management, allowing styles to be defined, referenced, and applied across various components. The struct is designed to be integrated with rendering functions, style managers, or theming systems to facilitate dynamic and uniform text appearance. Its straightforward structure supports easy extension and adaptation within larger styling architectures.
color_type.notes: This struct follows a simple data container pattern, emphasizing ease of use and integration with rendering and style management systems. The `bold` member is typically used as a boolean flag (0 or 1), but is stored as an integer for flexibility or future extension.
color_type.rationale: The `color_type` struct exists to standardize and encapsulate styling attributes for text elements, promoting consistency and reusability across the system. It serves as a fundamental building block within the styling architecture, enabling centralized management of visual styles and facilitating dynamic appearance adjustments in user interfaces or text rendering modules.
color_type.name: color_type

name.brief: Holds the textual identifier for a specific color type.
name.details: The `name` variable stores the name associated with a color type, serving as its primary identifier within the system. It is initialized with a string value that can originate from user input, configuration files, or predefined constants, ensuring flexibility in how color types are defined and referenced. This variable is utilized for display purposes, comparison operations (including case-insensitive matching thanks to the extended `String` class), and serialization of color type objects. Its design facilitates easy identification and manipulation of color types across various components of the application.
name.notes: Since `name` uses a custom `String` class with enhanced comparison features, it supports more robust and user-friendly matching operations, especially in scenarios involving user input or external data sources.
name.rationale: The `name` variable exists to provide a human-readable and programmatically accessible identifier for each color type, enabling consistent referencing, comparison, and display throughout the system.
name.name: name

code.brief: Holds the textual identifier of a color, such as a name or code.
code.details: The 'code' variable stores the color's identifier as a String object, which may contain hexadecimal values, color names, or other string representations. It is initialized with a specific string value and can be compared case-insensitively thanks to the extended features of the String class. This variable is used throughout the application to reference and display colors consistently, linking the textual code to visual properties. Its design allows for flexible handling of color identifiers in various contexts, such as rendering or user interface interactions.
code.notes: Utilizes the custom String class to facilitate case-insensitive comparisons and advanced string operations, ensuring reliable identification regardless of case variations.
code.rationale: This variable exists to provide a reliable and flexible way to identify colors by their textual codes, supporting consistent referencing, comparison, and display of color information within the system.
code.name: code

color.brief: An integer representing the color attribute of an object or element.
color.details: The `color` variable stores a numerical value that specifies the color associated with a particular object or visual element. It is initialized during object construction or configuration phases and can be updated at runtime to reflect changes in appearance. This variable is integral to rendering routines and interacts with color management functions to determine how the object is displayed on the screen.
color.notes: Typically stored as an integer code corresponding to predefined color constants or palette indices, enabling efficient color management and rendering.
color.rationale: This variable exists to facilitate flexible and dynamic visual representation of objects, allowing their colors to be set, modified, and used consistently across rendering functions.
color.name: color

bold.brief: An integer flag indicating whether the text should be rendered in bold style.
bold.details: The `bold` variable holds a value that specifies whether the associated text should be displayed in a bold font. It is usually set during style configuration, based on user input or formatting rules, and influences rendering decisions to apply the bold style accordingly. This variable interacts with other style attributes to determine the final text appearance during display.
bold.notes: Typically, `bold` is used as a boolean flag (e.g., 0 for false, non-zero for true), but it is stored as an integer to accommodate potential future style variations or additional states.
bold.rationale: This variable exists to control the application of bold styling in text rendering, enabling dynamic and configurable text appearance based on style settings or user preferences.
bold.name: bold
